title: About
text: I’ve spent several years doing research, writing code, and thinking about questions of human choice and decision making. Where I’ve studied and worked is in my résumé — this space is for the thinking.

title: About
text:
Why this, why now?
I read a lot of posts and keep catching myself asking the same thing: why should I trust this? What has the writer actually done, and what analysis stands behind the claim? That question is a large part of why I started writing. I’d rather show the work — the method, the data, the reasoning — than hand over a tidy takeaway and ask you to take my word for it.
I also learn better when I have to explain something to someone other than myself. So this is partly selfish: writing carefully is how I come to understand things properly.

title: About
text:
What is this about?
A space for the curious — people looking for answers to questions we have all run into that don’t quite scratch the itch, who want the finer details of the methods and the results, not just the headline.

title: About
text:
What to expect
A post most Fridays, on a topic in economics, econometrics, statistics, machine learning, artificial intelligence, or computer science. Each one is either a description of a method — how to apply it, when to apply it, why it matters now — or an analysis of a real question worked through with data. I try to make them worth reading: rigorous, but not a slog. Everything is free.

title: The Method Section
text:
Every post here is one of two kinds: a method — how a technique works and when to reach for it — or an applied question worked through with data. Two fields, one standard: show the work.

Economics & econometrics — filter with the Economics chip below.
Computer science & methods — filter with the Computer Science chip below.
Evaluating my work? — my résumé (PDF) has the formal background; the posts themselves are the better sample of how I think.

The category chips below (Economics · Computer Science · Method · Applied) filter the archive along either axis — topic or type of post. They appear as soon as posts are published.
Following along: new posts land on Fridays. Subscribe with any RSS reader via the feed, or plug that feed into a service like Blogtrottr for email — no account with me required.

title: The Method Section
text:
Recent posts


    
      
      
    





Selection and Insertion Sorts



Algorithms

Method



The two simplest sorts — how they work, and why they still earn their keep.





Jul 10, 2026

7 min




No matching items

title: Selection and Insertion Sorts
text: Sorting is one of the most basic approaches to solving problems in human thought. Be it prioritizing which tasks to tackle first or who to pay first; ranking in competitions, exams, and any other comparative decisions — we are sorting. It is a fundamental problem in computer science too: organizing records by date, tabulating results into a sequence of some kind. In this post we look at two of the simplest sort algorithms — selection sort and insertion sort. They are instructive, and although less efficient than other algorithms on large, randomly ordered data, they remain widely used at small sizes and on streaming data. The goal here is to introduce both and, in a companion post, put one to work on a real puzzle.

title: Selection and Insertion Sorts
text:
Selection Sort
The idea: repeatedly select the smallest remaining value and place it in the next position — smallest into slot 0, next-smallest into slot 1, and so on until every element is placed.

PseudocodePython


SELECTION-SORT(a):
    n ← length(a)
    for i ← 0 to n-1:
        min ← i
        for j ← i+1 to n-1:
            if a[j] < a[min]:
                min ← j
        swap a[i] and a[min]      # at most n-1 swaps total
    return a



def selection_sort(a):
    n = len(a)
    for i in range(n):
        smallest = i
        for j in range(i + 1, n):
            if a[j] < a[smallest]:
                smallest = j
        a[i], a[smallest] = a[smallest], a[i]   # pythonic swap, no temp needed
    return a

original = [5, 3, 4, 1, 2, 10, 1]
print("input: ", original)
print("sorted:", selection_sort(original.copy()))   # .copy(): it sorts in place

input:  [5, 3, 4, 1, 2, 10, 1]
sorted: [1, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 10]





The distinctive property: selection sort makes at most n − 1 swaps, the fewest of the elementary sorts. That barely matters in RAM, but it matters a lot when a write is the expensive operation.

title: Selection and Insertion Sorts
Selection and Insertion Sorts
text:
How they scale
Claims about running time are cheap; let’s measure. The two functions above are timed below on three kinds of input — randomly shuffled, already sorted, and reversed — across growing sizes, taking the best of a few trials at each size.


Show the benchmark code
import time, random
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def best_ms(sort, data, trials=3):
    best = float("inf")
    for _ in range(trials):
        d = data.copy()                 # fresh copy: the sorts mutate in place
        t = time.perf_counter()
        sort(d)
        best = min(best, time.perf_counter() - t)
    return best * 1000

sizes = [100, 200, 400, 800, 1600]
cases = {
    "random":   lambda n: random.sample(range(n * 3), n),
    "sorted":   lambda n: list(range(n)),
    "reversed": lambda n: list(range(n, 0, -1)),
}
random.seed(0)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9, 3.6), sharey=True)
for ax, (name, algo) in zip(axes, [("Selection sort", selection_sort),
                                   ("Insertion sort", insertion_sort)]):
    for label, gen in cases.items():
        ax.plot(sizes, [best_ms(algo, gen(n)) for n in sizes],
                marker="o", label=label)
    ax.set_title(name)
    ax.set_xlabel("input size n")
    ax.grid(alpha=0.3)
axes[0].set_ylabel("time (ms)")
axes[1].legend(title="input order")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Running time vs. input size. Both panels share a y-scale, so the gaps are real.





Two things jump out. Selection sort’s three curves lie almost on top of one another — it doesn’t care whether the input is sorted, shuffled, or reversed, because it always scans the entire unsorted remainder and always makes the same n − 1 swaps. Input order is invisible to it.
Insertion sort fans out completely. On sorted input it is essentially flat: the break fires on the first comparison of every pass, the work is linear, and at n = 1600 it runs on the order of a hundred times faster than selection sort. On random input it is quadratic and slightly worse than selection (more data movement). On reversed input — its worst case, where the break never fires — it is the slowest line on the whole plot. The same algorithm is both the fastest and the slowest curve in the figure, decided entirely by the order of its input. That is what adaptive means, made visible.

title: Selection and Insertion Sorts
section: Where these still earn their keep
text:
Where these still earn their keep


Insertion sort is online. It can absorb elements one at a time as they arrive, keeping the array sorted after each insertion — so it fits streaming input, where a divide-and-conquer sort would have to wait for the whole batch.
Insertion sort is adaptive. On nearly-sorted input the inner loop hits the break almost immediately, so it approaches O(n). This is why real-world hybrid sorts (like the Timsort behind Python’s own sorted) fall back to insertion sort on short runs, where its low overhead beats an asymptotically faster algorithm.
Selection sort minimizes writes. Its n − 1 swaps make it attractive when writing is costly or wearing — the seed of the companion post’s puzzle.

Next: an applied post that takes the “fewest exchanges” idea and runs with it — sorting a deck of cards laid face down, where every exchange is the costly move.
